filename: Case study classic bodybuilding peak week.pdf
source: Case study classic bodybuilding peak week.pdf
strategy: agentic chunking via Gemini (headline + summary + original text)

pages.text:
CLASSIC BODYBUILDING PEAK WEEK

This document contains a case study of the peak week protocol of one of Menno's natural classic
bodybuilding clients. It's reasonably representative for how most peak week protocols for men's
bodybuilding classes end up. Importantly, this is the finalized version of half a dozen trial runs to
optimize the carbload intake, figure out that cutting water wasn't worth it as long as sodium intake
was limited, and find the ideal shitload foods.

Days 1-3

You can adhere to your regular calories and protein intake, but reduce your carbs to ~100
g. Stick to foods from the safe, nonstarchy carbs, protein and fat list to fill in your macros
(see appendix).

Day 4: depletion

Record your caliper measurements today.

Glycogen depletion workout
Today's workout will be replaced by a glycogen depletion workout. You can perform your
regular exercises for that day. For set 1, go 1 rep to failure with an intensity as low as you
feel is still strength work (generally roughly 12 RM for isolation work and 8 RM for
compound work). Then reduce the weight to something you can perform 12-30 reps with
and perform your regular amount of sets minus 1 to a minimum of 1 (e.g. 4 sets becomes 3
sets, 1 set stays 1 set), staying in that rep range every set. The exact weights don't matter
much for this depletion workout. The first set makes sure the primary stimulus is anabolic.
Then you just try to get a ton of volume in for each body part.

This should be like a bro bodybuilding pump workout where you only perform the midrange
of all exercises. Performing only the middle part of each exercise's ROM maximizes work
output and metabolic stress, thereby maximizing glycolysis. You want to avoid overloading
your muscles in their stretched position anyway to minimize muscle damage, which impairs
glycogen resynthesis. For the same reason, the rep tempo should be explosive with no
emphasis on the eccentric contractions.

Add the following arm ergometer and bike ergometer glycogen depletion protocol after
your regular training session:
• Choose an intensity/cadence that you can maintain for a 15-60 second sprint.
• Rest 1-3 minutes between sets: only rest until you've caught your breath.
• Repeat this set until you can no longer achieve the same performance level. Digital
cardio machines are helpful here, as they can show your pace/intensity/work output.
• Lower the intensity/cadence by 10% and again keep doing sets like this until you can
no longer maintain performance. It's quite possible this only takes a single set.
• Repeat lowering the intensity like that 3 more times. This depletion work may take
10+ sets in total.

Make sure you've used both ergometers before, even if only for relatively light cardio work
on a day beforehand this week, to avoid excessive muscle soreness that will interfere with
glycogen supercompensation.

This session is going to be exhausting, so you may want to take a few minutes of rest after
your regular training session.

If you get extremely lightheaded or nauseous, like you're about to faint, that's a sign you're
'hitting the wall' and running out of glycogen in that muscle. You can stop then for that body
part.

The carbload
Immediately after your depletion workout, we start your carbload, which replaces your
remaining macros of today (meals 2 and 3) and your first meal tomorrow (brunch). In total,
the carbload should contain the following.

• 7387 calories
• Your regular amount of protein for all meals or more.
• A minimal intake of fat.
• Fructose has to stay under 100 g total. Consume only carbs from the safe foods lists
(both kinds: see appendix).

Start the carbload immediately post-workout with a shake containing 20+ g of (whey) protein
and 150+ g of carbs (dextrose/maltodextrose or anything else that contains a minimal
amount of fructose).

Make sure you consume plenty of fluids to attract as much intramuscular water as possible.
This means you should still consume a healthy amount of veggies and whole foods, not just
processed crap. Your sodium intake can remain normal (so absolute intake will rise in
proportion to energy intake).

You can send me a food log of your carbload to see how it can be improved so that you
won't bloat up excessively.

Day 5: continue carbload

Your first meal today is still part of your carbload. Your next 2 meals are PSMF as normal
for a rest day. From now until your pump-up backstage, you should not engage in any more
high intensity exercise, including strenuous posing.

Stick to foods from the safe, nonstarchy carbs, protein and fat list to fill in your macros (see
appendix).

Day 6: Physique competition

Record your caliper measurements today.

Water intake: regular by thirst.

Sodium intake: conservative. Regular by taste but without any high sodium (processed) foods
or sauces. Safe option: stick with veggies and meat, poultry or fish.

You can adhere to your regular training day calories and protein intake, but limit your carbs
to ~100 g, not including the shitloads below. Stick to foods from the safe, nonstarchy carbs,
protein and fat list to fill in your macros (see appendix). Stick to your regular meal times,
unless this would mean you have a meal within 3 hours of your shitload.

Avoid all stimulants on the day of your contest. They are vasoconstricting and will thus limit
vascularity and your pumps.

Shitload
1 g sodium, 100 g carbs, 25 g fat, 1 consumption of alcohol (optional) 90 minutes before the
prejudging and finals.
Safe option: 4 rice crackers with 1 tblspn Nutella each, half a teaspoon of table salt and 50 ml
of spirit alcohol (e.g. vodka).

If 4+ hours pass between the shitload for the prejudging and the finals, consume a second
shitload for the finals, again 90 mins. before going on stage with no other meals in between.
Otherwise, consume only a shitload before the prejudging and consume another 100 g of
carbs in the form of rice crackers directly after the prejudging (nothing added).

Pump-up
To maximize muscle fullness and vascularity, you want to get 'pumped' before each shot to
induce swelling and vascularity in your muscles. Here's how.

• Volume: Stay in the 12 - 50 rep range. Don't do more sets than needed to get a
good pump, as you'll then just excessively deplete glycogen levels. Keep your rest
intervals short if you do more than one set per muscle group.
• Execution: Controlled eccentrics, explosive concentrics. If it's an isolation exercise,
focus your mind on the muscle that you want to get pumped.
• ROM: Keep the tension on your muscles. This generally means avoiding completely
locked out and stretched positions.
• Exercises: push-ups, banded lateral raises with a shrug at the top, banded curls and
banded lat prayers.

Appendix: Safe Foods List
Orange colored foods are tolerated well by most people but not everyone. Only consume
these if you've tried them before and you can say with great confidence you don't
experience any digestive problems from them. As a general rule, it's safe to stick with foods
you know you tolerate and not to experiment with large quantities of novel foods.

Safe, nonstarchy carbs
• Low FODMAP, nonstarchy vegetables:
o Most non-cruciferous green vegetables: green beans, alfalfa, cucumber,
bamboo shoots, lettuce (endive, escarole, iceberg lettuce, leafy varieties,
radicchio, romaine), spinach, (Swiss) chard/silverbeet, seaweed (incl. nori),
chives, parsley and green spring onion/scallions
o Most root vegetables: carrots, celery, parsley, jicama/Mexican yambeans,
radishes and parsnip
o Certain cruciferous vegetables: bok choy, pak choi/Chinese cabbage, choy
sum, rocket/arangula, broccoli, radish, turnip, kale and zucchini
o okra, endive, squashes and pumpkins
o eggplant/aubergine
o chilies and peppers
o tomato
• All fermented vegetables: sauerkraut, kimchi, pickles, curtido, beet kvass, kombucha,
water kefir, coconut kefir, etc.
• Low FODMAP fruits:
o berries (all except blackberries)
o papaya/pawpaw, cantaloupe melon/rockmelon and honeydew melon (nót
watermelon!)
o chayote
o grapefruit
o carambola/starfruit
o lemon, lime
• Low FODMAP spices/seasoning: basil, chili, cardamom, coriander, paprika, cinnamon,
cumin, anise, saffron, ginger, lemongrass, marjoram, mint, oregano, parsley, rosemary

and thyme, traditionally fermented soy sauce products (incl. oyster sauce, miso and
fish sauce), mustard and vinegar

Safe, starchy carbs
• Low FODMAP but sugary fruits:
o grapes
o oranges, mandarins/tangerines and tangelos/honeybells
o bananas
o durian
o kiwifruit/Chinese gooseberries and pitaya (dragon fruit)
o passion fruit
o pineapple
o tamarind
o breadfruit
o guava
o lychee
• Low FODMAP or fermented starches: White rice (all kinds), yams, (sweet) potatoes,
tapioca, yucca/cassava/manioc, lotus root, taro, traditionally fermented sourdough
bread (most commercial varieties are not sufficiently fermented!), plantains, rutabagas
and all derivatives of the above products, such as rice/potato flour.

Safe protein sources
• Fermented or lactose-free dairy and dairy derived protein powder
• Tempeh and traditionally prepared tofu from fermented soy beans
• Unprocessed poultry, fish and meat
• Eggs (well done, not raw)

Safe fat sources
• Whole food unprocessed animal fats
• Oils

• Butters

Double check to avoid
• Garlic, onions, artichoke, asparagus, mushrooms, shallots and leek bulbs
• Legumes (incl. beans and nuts) and wheat
• Sugar alcohols
• Apples, apricots, plums, plumes, prunes, watermelon, nectarine and cherries
